# Poisson regression: counting fish near logging

Counts — fish on a transect, eggs in a nest, individuals in a quadrat — are not
Gaussian. They are non-negative integers, and their variance grows with their
mean. The **Poisson** distribution is the natural starting point.

## The data: a coral-reef nursery under pressure

`fish-counts.csv` comes from surveys of juvenile **bumphead parrotfish**
(*Bolbometopon muricatum*) at 49 reef sites in **Kia Province, Solomon
Islands**. The bumphead is the largest parrotfish in the world and is both
culturally and ecologically important — but its juveniles depend on healthy
branching *Acropora* coral as nursery habitat.

Across the bay, terrestrial **logging** washes sediment onto the reefs. Sediment
smothers the branching coral the young fish shelter in, so the concern is that
sites closer to logging operations support fewer juveniles. Each row is one site:
`fish` is the number of juvenile bumpheads counted there, and
`dist_to_logging_km` is the site's distance from the nearest logging operation,
in kilometres.

> **Data & attribution.** Hamilton, R.J., Almany, G.R., Brown, C.J., Pita, J.,
> Peterson, N.A. & Choat, J.H. (2017) *Logging degrades nursery habitat for an
> iconic coral reef fish.* **Biological Conservation** 210: 273–280.
> <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2017.04.025>. The copy used here has been
> lightly modified for teaching with FANGS.

## The model

We cannot model the expected count $\lambda$ directly with a straight line,
because a line can go negative. Instead we model it on the **log scale** — the
**log link**:

$$
\text{fish}_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i), \qquad
\log(\lambda_i) = \alpha + \beta\,\text{dist}_i
$$

In FANGS:

```
model {
  for (i in 1:N) {
    fish[i] ~ dpois(lambda[i])
    log(lambda[i]) <- alpha + beta * dist_to_logging_km[i]
  }
  alpha ~ dnorm(0, 10)
  beta  ~ dnorm(0, 10)
}
```

## Interpreting on the log scale

Coefficients act **multiplicatively** on the count. A slope $\beta$ means each
extra kilometre from logging multiplies the expected count by $e^{\beta}$. A
**positive** $\beta$ therefore means *more* juvenile fish the further you get
from logging — the statistical footprint of sediment damage on the nursery.

## Your task

Load `fish-counts.csv`, fit the Poisson model, and read the *Summary* tab. Enter
your posterior means for $\alpha$ and $\beta$ (both on the log scale). Then ask
yourself: does the **sign** of $\beta$ support the idea that logging harms the
nursery, and how many extra fish per kilometre does $e^{\beta}$ imply?
